Along with that spectacular imagery, there's some fascinating science going on in the Orion Nebula. Binary planet pairs, these ones < 7 x Jovian masses, just floating around in space not associated with any host star. Like when otters hold hands & float. 🔭🧪 📸 Pre-print: arxiv.org/abs/2310.01231

Battle of the armored beasts. The huge crocodile-relative Barinasuchus is trying to find a way to tackle the glyptodont Boreostemma in Miocene northern South America Day 5 of my October ink-marker sketches 🐡 🎨 🧪 #paleontology
Move out of the way! Here comes Tarbosaurus Day 7 of October ink-marker sketches 🐡 🎨 🧪 #paleontology

Bored in Rona isolation on a Saturday night, so decided to reprocess a few of my monochromes. 2020/2021 captures from my 8” SCT in my Bortle 9 Sydney backyard using Alt-Az mount with 10 sec subs, for total of 1 hour each. Tell me … how do you like your spirals?! 🌀🔭🧪

Australian geology from space appreciation thread. Look at these fantastic structures in the very old rock we have at the centre of our continent. See that eye-like structure in the centre, that's Gosses Bluff, a ~142 Mya (Jurrasic-Cretaceous) meteorite impact crater 🧪 📸 Google Maps 1/3

Zooming in a bit (Google Earth resolution is so good these days) and we get a better view of the huge impact crater. The outer ring, which once would have been about 22 km in diameter has since been eroded down, and the inner 5-km mountain ring is all that is left. 🧪 📸 Google Maps 2/3

I've been to Tyler's Pass (where this pic was taken, not mine) to see it. It's remarkable how it emerges from the surrounding flats. We didn't get closer as it is on Indigenous land and holds sacred cultural significance to the Western Arrernte people. It is beautiful to see. 🧪 📸 ExploreOz 3/3

I think this is my favorite astrophotography project so far. 🔭🧪 Lion Nebula mosaic, captured in four colors: * Deep red: Sulphur-II * Red-Orange: Hydrogen-alpha * Green-blue: Oxygen-III * Deep blue: Hydrogen-beta Tech details: www.astrobin.com/6dr79e/ 38 hours of photons in Indiana.
Squabble in the air A pair of Muzquizopteryx fighting. Day 10 of my ink-marker sketches. I’m REALLY happy with this one! 🐡 🎨 🧪 #paleontology

Gorgeous before and after image of the southern galaxy, NGC 1097 (in Fornax) showing the recent supernova that I was excited about a few weeks back. This is a core-collapse supernova, so a black hole or neutron star likely formed! A single star/event, outshining billions 🌟💥 📸 APOD/B. Miller 🔭🧪

Captured the pretty dazzling lights of excited high-altitude oxygen for the first time. It was spectacular. A reminder that Earth really is the best spaceship we have. And we should care for it. 🔭🧪

The DES Galaxy Collection: No. 3! 🔭🧪 Name: NGC7814 Category: Dusty Distance: ~40 mly away Constellation: Pegasus Interesting: This almost edge-on beaut has a little warp and twist that is detected in optical, and the behind dust lane can just be seen. Also, lots of background galaxies! 📸DES Legacy

Have you met the neighbours yet? From a dark sky, and using a simple DSLR, you can capture five galaxies, which make up part of our lil’ local cluster. The Milky Way (of course), the Magellanic Clouds visible to the eye and M31 + M33 to the camera. Graphic by SkyFlubbler #Astrophotography 🔭🧪

Out there in the deep cold of outer space, beyond the orbit of Neptune, something wonderful is happening. Tonight, as it reaches its aphelion, Halley's Comet begins banking back towards Earth. Mark your calendars for 6/9/61 when an old friend returns. 🧪 🔭 ☄️ www.space.com/halleys-come...

The DES Galaxy Collection: No. 6! 🔭🧪 Name: NGC7020 Category: Put a ring on it Distance: ~138 mly away Constellation: Pavo Interesting: This early-type beaut has a detached outer ring where most of the star formation is occurring and an inner ring with pointy hexagonal-like structures. 📸 DES Legacy

Astronomer Annie Jump Cannon was born OTD in 1863. She was a pioneer of stellar classification and co-creator of the Harvard Classification Scheme. Over her lifetime she *manually* classified around 350,000 stars. 🧪 🔭 👩‍🔬 Image: Harvard University, Radcliffe Archives

this little piece of rock is not only as old as the solar system itself, making it 4.56 BILLION years old it probably also contains grains of material that formed BEFORE the solar system even came into existence 🤯🧪

Arrgghh, there is so much beauty beyond our atmosphere (don't get too sappy, everything in space wants to kill us). But look at this beautiful image capture by Damian Peach, catching C/2020 V2 (ZTF) in front of a trio of Galaxies in the constellation Grus. 🔭🧪 📸 www.damianpeach.com/deepsky/c202...

That HUGE x-class solar flare that went off a few days back is impressive. The most powerful since 2017. 🔭🧪 Question for any helio-science folks on here: Looking at NASA SDO filters, the timestamp on the flares is in the 10-min range - does it take this long to propagate through solar atmosphere?

actions.action_text: One time I weirded out a nice guy who gave me his number because I kept obsessing about the number itself. I couldn’t figure out why so I asked for his pen and scribbled out equations. Then I got it. His number formed a perfect right triangle. I called later and left a message but never heard back.

actions.target: Are you kidding?! That's AMAZING
actions.action_text: THANK YOU. I was so proud to show him. The area code was the short leg. The next three were the long leg. The final four numbers were the hypotenuse. All nice and square. He feigned being impressed and rolled his eyes. I thought (hoped?) he was playing. Oh well.

actions.target: Can you still remember the number tho? I’d say that was cool.
actions.action_text: Thanks. He was not impressed, though. I do not remember the number. But it was very, very square.

actions.target: So, you know how JWST took an image of the Crab Nebula recently … well, that data is already available! Downloaded them last night and have processed them today. Here’s my version of the inside of the Crab Nebula, powered by the mighty pulsar in the centre! 📸 NASA, ESA, CSA, STScI, T. Temim 🔭🧪

If we look carefully near the centre, we can see these horizontal plane rings - that’s caused by the pulsar’s magnetosphere and rotation. It dumps A LOT of energy into the surrounding nebula. The pulsar itself is tiny, only ~20km across, but it illuminates its surroundings 33 times per second! 🔭🧪

actions.target: something that transformed my idea of forgiveness is when i realized that all that talk about how good forgiveness feels—how it sets you free, etc. —is bunk. forgiveness is hard and it sucks. it's relinquishing your right to satisfaction. it's not something we do for ourselves. we do it for others.
actions.action_text: There's a lovely moment in the film Philomena that really drove this home for me--when Judi Dench's character chooses to forgive someone who really doesn't deserve it at all. It's not really a triumphant moment or even a happy moment. Forgiveness takes a tremendous amount of courage.

actions.target: Em setembro tive uma epifania: E se, além das aulas, eu criasse um material educacional divertido? Brinquedos e jogos pras crianças, livros para alfabetização e ampliações de conhecimento, vídeos divertidos sobre cultura e história, passeios turísticos culturais, focando sempre em empatia e vivência

Durante os últimos meses desenvolvi 10 bonecos, os quais os protótipos estão prontos e os detalhes da primeira coleção (Brasil - 5 bonecos) estão sendo finalizados e logo estarão liberados para encomendas.Hoje começa o primeiro passo da oficialização deste sonho, pois, como disse, comprei o domínio!

Estou muito empolgada e orgulhosa desse projeto, e espero muito que ele seja tão legal quanto ele é na minha cabeça. Pois ele me permitirá alcançar mais gente, estimulando o conhecimento de mundo e de vivência, a tolerância e o respeito ao próximo. Então peço desculpas se eu me tornar monotemática.

Did you know ebooks are also an accessibility tool?

I have dyslexia, when I read paper books I find them incredibly fatiguing, the words look like they've got old TV static where I'm not directly focusing.

So I read everything on an e-reader. It allows me to turn any book into large print.
actions.action_text: I found the dyslexic font on my nook easier for my ADHD brain to concentrate on. 😊 I wonder if the battery runs out on yours faster because of the increased number of page turns, or if the smaller amount of e-ink being used balances that out? 🤔

actions.target: I've done a couple of radio shows way back when with Dickenson. He's not a bad guy. The thing with folks like him and Daltrey on Brexit: 1) they come from a time of managers sorting all the logistics so never had to deal with work permits / visas, 2) they truly still believe in Anglo exceptionalism.
actions.action_text: 1) Managers still sort this kind of stuff for bands the size of Iron Maiden. And Iron Maiden has toured the whole world, it's hardly the first time they need a visa. 2) With his level of access to information, he shouldn't hold this view anymore. If he does, he is a bellend.

actions.target: It's probable that the problems he is complaining about are related to his solo touring plans. In Brazil last year they had difficulty booking an orchestra, of the sort that Iron Maiden's management would hardly let get near his ears.
actions.action_text: As it was pointed out already, many people tried to warn him about the impact of Brexit in arts. "But his bubble" - he has access to information and still insists in British exceptionalism. He is a very educated person who should know better but prefers to think he is special. So: a bellend.

actions.target:
Those are the points I was trying to make, yes. And he's so far up the ladder, he always will have people sorting it.

I'm not excusing him. He should know better. But many people live in various kinds of bubbles.
actions.action_text: Either he always has people sorting this stuff for him or he is a guy now having to deal with things for his solo career (which I highly doubt). At least choose your excuse. P.S. it def sounds like you are excusing him since your first post.

actions.target:
I don't know his specifics, but I'd reckon that touring with Iron Maiden, he probably talks to the same 10 or 12 people every day for the entire time they're on tour. It get's very insular, from my experience, unless you really make and effort.

I'm not defending Dickenson, by the way. 1/
actions.action_text: He should be making an effort then.

actions.target: I long for connection, intimacy, relational and sexual healing from decades of fear and repression, but all I have to show for it right now is a tiny little life, flying solo, making no waves. 😮‍💨
actions.action_text: :hug: I'm sure things will turn around! How long has it been since you came out?

actions.target: 2 3/4 years, which I know isn't really that long, but it certainly feels it, especially the older I get.
actions.action_text: True, but it can take a while to find your people, especially if you're struggling with self esteem issues. Apps are nice for making easy connections but they can be dehumanizing. It definitely helps to get out locally too. :-)

actions.target: Yeah, I'm okay if a local LGBT group and that's been a lifesaver and I've made some good friends through it, including people who, for the first time in my life, I don't doubt if they actually like me, but I don't see them that often and that's largely on me. Object permanence etc.
actions.action_text: That's good! Are there any gay gaming groups near you too? Those are usually pretty active communities.

actions.target: No. It's a pretty small town so modt of the more specific LGBT groups are in the cities, at least half an hour (and a small fortune) away by train.
actions.action_text: Ah, that makes it tough. :-( You might have some luck with online gay gaming communities (I'll search myself a little later). It's not quite what you're looking for, but you never know what you might find! And you'd be less at the mercy of the algorithm haha.

actions.target: Yeah, I've recently got more involved in a UK Gaymer Facebook group. Difficult to actually get to know anyone in it, though, especially if, like me, you're not a big competitive multiplayer type.
actions.action_text: Yeah, I'm not into competitive shooters myself, which is limiting. Do they have a group for Among Us? Or Fall Guys?

Standard start-up sell-line: "Bringing innovation to the field of document storage!" Service offered: printing out paper copies of classified documents and burning them to power the Chaos engine.

Another example: in "The Kiss Hello," Jerry is called into action to open a ketchup bottle for his nana, which leads to an extended riff regarding squeezable mustard vs. squeezable ketchup, and it's a shame that squeezable ketchup doesn't exist. I do not remember a time WITHOUT squeezable ketchup.

I think there's an interesting psychology *zizeksniff* at play with what IT tools are expected to report and what they actually do report that is either actively antagonistic or severely misunderstands human behavior, both of which seem equally plausible.

Case in point! Ask how many people are on site? Well, instead you know the subset of freaks who religiously open a new browser window every day and DON'T inadvertently stop the 15+ second loading of an intranet site with multiple redirects.

actions.target: I feel this post in my guts only because a) I can never get to my kids' laundry because b) my dryer takes four cycles to dry one fucking load
actions.action_text: Have you ever had your dryer vents cleaned? That's definitely not normal in a "might be a fire hazard" way.

actions.target: I am fastidious about cleaning the lint trap. one time I complained to my landlord about it, they checked the vents, said they were good, and then told I put too much in, meanwhile, they're same size I'd put in the dryer at my now-ex's house and sometimes it takes two cycles for ONE PAIR OF JEANS
actions.action_text: It wouldn't be the trap, it's the vent from the dryer to the outside. If the heating element is working, but you're not venting the humid air, the dryer is going to be much less efficient -- which sounds like exactly what's happening. And that buildup in the vent isn't good.

actions.target:
Yeah I wouldn't trust what the landlord says. This sounds exactly like an issue with the dryer exhaust. If you know where it let's out you can get a drill brush and go at it. Or get a pro to do it for not much money.

Even with annual cleanings the difference onces it's been cleaned is stark.
actions.action_text: Haha yeah "don't trust a landlord" is always a good bet. Honestly even the electric or gas savings if you're paying out of pocket would be worth it.

actions.target: The problem is I dunno who's worse, the small business tyrant landlord or the corporate one. But I have the latter and I don't like it lol 🙃
actions.action_text: The corporate ones at least usually have full time repairmen and more legal visibility than small local landlords, but on the topic of landlords in general, I'm a staunch Maoist.

Eu tenho uma história com o MST ❤️
Pra quem não sabe, eu fui estudante de geografia na USP. Eu até então nunca tinha ido a um assentamento. Na real era bem ignorante e achava que era simplesmente um bando de vagabundo. Não me orgulho disso. Mas foi assim que eu aprendi.
actions.action_text: Meu pai foi aluno de geografia da facul particular daqui e eles iam pra lugares privados, acho que se ele tivesse tido sua experiência ele não teria caído tão feio pro bozonarismo. Minha família sempre foi de reaças e sempre tiveram esses pensamentos reforçados pelo nosso ambiente de interior

actions.target: Infelizmente isso é muito reflexo da falta de educação no sentido literal. O trabalho do MST no campo é lindo. Você ver a terra improdutiva crescer, alimentar e produzir para as famílias é maravilhoso 🥰
actions.action_text: mas é isso mesmo, o pior é que essas pessoas desse interior tem orgulho demais pra admitir que são ignorantes, aqui tem uma relação coronelista no psicologico das pessoas mto parecida com o fenomeno do pobre de direita, principalmente pq os "coroneis" estão mto proximos fisicamente da gente comum

actions.target: What is a "rss feed"
actions.action_text: very very old internet that most websites still post their content too. it gives you a pure non filtered list of whatever the source is. for example, the verge. i can pull their rss feed and every article on their website would show up in real time. it can be customized with a reader

actions.target: Jesus, how does one access an RSS
actions.action_text: there’s like a million rss readers. the links themselves just look like unformatted sites. some readers are automatic and pull down feeds, others require input. currently what i’m doing is using tfttt to automate rss posting to discord. turning discord into a diy reader 😂

There was a long time when I wanted to read more fiction but I had no idea what I’d like. 

⏩ to now when I’m trying to tear through the current novel I’m enjoying and get to the dozens of others on my list I’m excited about. 📚
actions.action_text: I find myself digging my heels in to slow down sometimes, so I can spend more time with the characters or in the world. It's usually not intentional, and whenever I notice that I'm doing it I have to comfort the fretful crow in my brain who is afraid the stories will run out. I can REREAD! HECC!

actions.target: Yeah, sometimes I need to tell myself it’s ok to slow down and savor. Every book reads a little differently. I’ve read some 300 page books in a day and some over two weeks. And like you said, if I like it I can always go back and find something new! I love re-reading favorites.
actions.action_text: Yeeeeeep! It's so hard sometimes though, I feel like I want to eat up the book, like reading it voraciously somehow makes my experience of it more visceral and real? I think the trick for me might be having the re-read or new read queued up and planned for immediately thereafter. hrm hrm hrmmmm,

actions.target: That’s what I do! It’s really important to me to read at least a few pages of the next book after I finish the previous one. Keep that excitement and momentum going
actions.action_text: YESSS! That's a brilliant idea, the pages read to keep it going. I'm going to adopt that rather than keeping to my usual vague feelings of, like, betraying the former book/s. Aheh.

actions.action_text: Salt the water HEAVILY. Oil the water or cooking pasta!? Nope. Never. No sauce will stick to that pasta EVER. No thank. I do, however, dip out a cup or two of the starchy pasta water near the end of the cooking time for whatever sauce I am gonna use.

actions.target: Many dino mysteries, though, are about natural history basics. Consider Torosaurus latus! This three-horned dinosaur was a close relative of Triceratops and lived in some of the same habitats 68-66 million years ago. It seems rare, but that rarity might have to do with the fossil record itself. 🧪

Paleontologists have worked out some of the backstory. Over multiple years, Allosaurus died on a floodplain. Their remains were washed into stream channels that overflowed into a pond. Dead dino soup. But what drew so many large carnivores to one place, season after season? That’s the question. 🧪

If you find fossils of a carnivorous dinosaur in the Morrison Formation, it’s likely to be Allosaurus fragilis. We know many fossils of this theropod, from eggs to 32 ft, 2 ton adults. But why were dozens of Allosaurus buried together at Cleveland-Lloyd Dinosaur Quarry in Utah? We don’t know yet. 🧪

Plenty has been written about what dinosaurs looked like or sounded like, but what about smell? What did non-avian dinosaurs smell like? I’m not sure how we would tell outside of “carnivores probably stank, big herbivores were fart machines,” but I still wonder about the Mesozoic scentscape. 🧪

Famous as western N. America is for its Late Cretaceous fossils, sea level drop and rising Rockies meant less habitat where dinosaurs could be preserved (even compared to 10 million years earlier). It can be hard to tell whether a “rare” dinosaur really was rare or mostly lived somewhere else. 🧪

By studying living relatives of ancient plants like horsetails and monkey puzzle trees, though, paleontologists have proposed that many Mesozoic plants were more digestible and nutritious than previously thought. It’s the plants that underwrite the lives of giants. 🧪

The basics of dinosaur biology are some of the most enduring mysteries. I’m often asked how much a big dinosaur like the Jurassic Diplodocus ate each day, but there are so many unknowns about their physiology and the plants they fed on. We can’t just walk up and look. 🧪

It’s hard to think of dinosaurs without imagining them roaring, grunting, honking, and generally making a ruckus. But with rare exception, we don’t know what non-avian dinosaurs sounded like. Even for Parasaurolophus, with its hollow headgear, it’s like having a trombone but not the music. 🧪

The dinosaur family tree was probably fluffier than we know. Feathers and their precursors have been found across Dinosauria, from close relatives to birds to lineages that were only distantly-related such as horned dinosaurs. That suggests fossil fluff could have been present in any dino lineage 🧪

Dinosaur inner ears can help provide some detail here. One study found that babies of dinosaurs like Parasaurolophus made higher-frequency calls, near the limit of what adults could hear, while the adults were suited to making and hearing low-frequency sounds. Honk. 🧪

It’d help if some dinosaurs were literally preserved dead in their tracks. Such fossils are the rarest of the rare. But sometimes a formation has only one dinosaur capable of making a certain track type. Giant theropod tracks in the latest Cretaceous of western N. America? Probably T. rex. 🧪

Many, if not most, dinosaur tracks represent unknown species. Good layers for tracks rarely have bones, and vice versa. When I spotted this Cretaceous footprint in Denali National Park I could immediately tell it was left by a hadrosaur from the proportions, but what species? We may never know. 🧪

And if you’d like to know more about dinosaur farts, there are some papers on that. The climate implications of this study have been disputed but it’s still fascinating to think about how big dinosaurs affected the global environment. 🧪 💨

Some kind of protofeather might have been an ancestral dinosaur feature, part of a suite of traits related to life at small size during the early Triassic. Protofeathers likely evolved for display and regulating body temperature first, modified into many forms throughout the Mesozoic. 🧪

A new study shows that flowers have evolved over the past three decades to have less sex—potentially in response to the disappearance of bees. It could be a death spiral for some species. Here’s my story. 🧪 Gift link: www.nytimes.com/2024/01/04/s...

The Moon rotates so it always shows Earth (almost) the same face - albeit in different illuminations! The first image we have of the farside of the Moon is this one from the Soviet Probe, Luna 3 in 1959. 🔭🧪👩🏻‍🔬

The Moon is an excellent (arguably the best) unaided eye target, and has been observed by cultures all over the world for thousands of years. In the west, stories of the “Man” or “Face” in the Moon abound, as illustrated in many story books - this one from around 1720. 🔭🧪👩🏻‍🔬

Kantha in Bengali means cloth, traditionally made from the best parts of old worn saris or dhotis. Also the name of a way of stitching in which variations of running stitch are worked though all the layers of cloth, quilting them together, creating intricate designs #WomensArt

Não estou generalizando pq não tenho como afirmar que é assim pra todo mundo. Mas talvez seja pra muitos. Especialmente em grandes cidades, em empregos menos flexíveis, etc. É um saco. Mas eu quero ter boas experiências TB, fazer coisas legais e que me alimentam a alma. O capitalismo é um cu

Not to be disgruntled renter on main but... Who the fuck vacuums hardwood floors?

Damn girl get a broom 😑
actions.action_text: We recently went back to the old-school canister style vacuum (obviously a new take on it though) that is primarily designed for non-carpeted floors, and it is a game changer for sure. Far more efficient and effective than a broom - but our whole house is hardwood, so that would be a lot of sweeping

actions.target: Yeah this is an 800sqft apartment.
actions.action_text: Sweeping really doesn't pick up everything though, and that's still a large apartment (imho!) Canister vacuums aren't really for carpeted homes either, so there are tools specifically made for this 😜 (But also yeah, we have 4 floors to vacuum cuz rowhouse. Anything that helps is desirable!)

actions.target: I can so get behind making cleaning as easy and efficient as possible. Don't get me wrong. Like I won't lie I kinda wanna get myself one of those cute little robots but also I am mildly phobic of smart tech. I just also know how hard refinishing wood floors is.
actions.action_text: For sure! That's why we went old-school haha. No smart tech here - it's absolutely unnecessary. Since it's designed for hardwood specifically, there are attachments that are gentle. No powered brushheads or anything like that (though there are two attachments for that too, for rugs or upholstery!)

How to people get over shame? 

I just realized that seems to be the big cause for holding people back from things. 

So I’m just looking for some insight from others perspective.
actions.action_text: Healthy Shame: "I did a bad thing. I feel bad about that." Toxic Shame: "I did a bad thing. I too must be bad." Too often when we are young, those two are not clearly explained. As a result, we get those two conflated. It's easy to do and can become an unhealthy pattern.

actions.action_text: it was very surreal to have like a straight up month where all the transit fare taking stuff didn't work. I feel infinitely sorry for the bus drivers and station people who suddenly had to be customer service for a broken outsourced scam.

actions.target: The city's hand was forced because the vendor who made the swipe cards we pivoted to after tokens sunset the technology. Where ventra really went wrong was putting Forrest Claypool in charge of the CTA. He thought Ventra was an opportunity to turn CTA cards into banking tools & failed spectacularly.
actions.action_text: if I recall correctly the terms of the banking were remarkably bad and predatory, but that's all fine because no one used it anyway.

actions.target: Claypool even pitched it as a way to bring banking to the unhoused in Chicago. Like sure, we don't have any public toilets, too few public beds, and your boss just closed most of the free mental health resources, but what people on the streets really need is a debit account on their bus pass.

actions.target: I’ll admit, I’m terrible about this. I’d immediately be like “why do you care about ______ so much?” to EVERYTHING. Until she understood I was not her audience. Maybe she can’t be changed, but she doesn’t need to be heard.
actions.action_text: I’ve rebuffed her remarks in the past and tried to respond in ways that make it clear I do not share her viewpoints without starting “work drama” but I’ve probably been too subtle. I also worry about how eager she seems for confrontation, to validate her sense of being censored/victimized.

actions.target: Fuck that noise. My favorite thing to do with this kind of person is start making them question their behaviors “Why don’t you like that person with blue hair? Why do you feel that way?” And just keep bringing it forward until they admit either a prejudice, an Aha moment, or at worst writing it off
actions.action_text: With a friend I’d be willing to have that conversation and do that work. I’d have the space and privacy to handle it with care. actions.action_text: Part of me does wonder, “What did I do? What did I say? Was I following the ‘wrong person’?” But, at the end of the day, it doesn’t matter. I’ve blocked because of behavior or statements I’ve found outrageously egregious. I’ve also blocked on a whim. Others are entitled to do the same.

9 years ago today, I was helping to work up this bull shark as part of a research expedition. 🧪🦑🌎🦈🐡 It’s on a floating jet ski dock we tie behind the boat, and it can breathe because of the water pump on its mouth. We collect data for several projects and safely release the animal rapidly.

Science on Bluesky is growing rapidly! Science friends, introduce yourselves below and I’ll share! Who are you? What do you work on? Where are you based? I’ll go first. I’m David, and I study sharks and their conservation. I’m based in the Washington, DC area. 🧪🦑🌎🦈🐡

Mike Mike Mike Mike Mike! Guess what day it is? Guess. What. Day. It. Is?!? That’s right, today is international sawfish day! These shark-like rays with chainsaw snoots are Critically Endangered, and today is about raising awareness. 🧪🦑🌎🐡🦈 www.sawfishconservationsociety.org/internationa...
What is common knowledge in your field, but shocks outsiders? There’s a new species of shark or shark relative (ray or chimaera) discovered somewhere in the world about every two weeks. 🧪🦑🐡
Interacting stressors: climate change & invasive species. More bad news for Australia's coral reefs. 🌏🧪

In which I post a daily shark fact 🦈🧪🦑 🌎 on Bluesky every day unless I forget in which case they’ll be more like daily-ish: Sharks absolutely get cancer. Even if they didn’t, eating shark doesn’t cure cancer, just like eating LeBron James doesn’t make you better at basketball.

actions.target: In which I post a daily shark fact 🦈🧪🦑 🌎 🐡on Bluesky every day unless I forget in which case they’ll be more like daily-ish: No matter how many times you hear it on Shark Week, no, bull sharks are absolutely not the only shark species that can enter fresh water.

Fascinating suggestion from biophysical analysis of 44 million year old amber fossil morphology, that ancient katydid insects could hear the frequencies used by early bats for echolocation. An ancient predator/prey arms race! 🧪 www.cell.com/current-biol...

It’s always sad when an animal dies, but by reporting it to researchers, the folks who found this shark did an incredible service. Multiple papers will be written about this specimen, and lots of books will need to be updated! 🧪🦑🌎🐡🦈

Got one of these today. Please know that if I ask you to summarize part of your paper in a way a 10YO can understand, it's not because I'm lazy. It's because I think your research will inspire kids. I want to quote you, so they can see you as a person and learn from you directly. 🧪
This long-snouted, ancient fish at the GarLab…isn’t a gar, but a juvenile Lake Sturgeon! 🐟🧪

🌈🐳 GAY WHALES 🐳🌈 In 2019 off the coast of Hawai'i, researchers recorded humpback whales having sex for the first time ever. Little is known about humpback sex, so this was notable enough! But in an excellent twist, it turned out that both whales were male 😎🧪 www.pacificwhale.org/pwf-research...

This is not really surprising! (it's just great to have it on camera) Gay behaviour has been documented in a bunch of whale and dolphin species, from orca and grey whales (which effectively have gay orgies), to river and bottlenose dolphins (which form lil' queer couples) 🐋🧪🐬

actions.target: I started getting panic attacks at 45. I got given some beta blockers to fight them and they worked really well. I used to panic about getting the panic attack so knowing that I could take a pill to make them go away means I don't panic about the panic attack any more and very rarely need the pill.
actions.action_text: Really? It’s been suggested to me before, but I thought they were for something else, so didn’t take it seriously, but I’m curious now. I take aspirin when I get anxious as it really relaxes me. My late step-grandfather used to after he had a heart attack and got hypertensive. Thank you x

Very common among gay men, I’ve found. Quite obvious why. I rarely discuss mine professionally. I just find my way of dealing with it effective, but it gets less so the older I get and I think that a because of how it physically manifests. Thank you Ant. You know how much I love you ❤️❤️😘

Nesse exato momento tá rolando um evento que eu chamaria de "bafômetro stop n' go (pare e siga)". Polícia rodoviária fazendo teste de bafômetro no meio da rodovia (aproveitando o trânsito parado), sem desviar os carros pro acostamento. Em Ubatuba, na Praia Grande Se beber, não dirija!!

Dito isso, foram 20 minutos pra cruzar a Praia Grande, sentido centro de Ubatuba pras praias do Sul e Caraguatatuba. No outro sentido (indo pro centro), o trânsito tá beeem pior, bem travado pelo menos desde o início da praia das Toninhas. (obviamente não tou dirigindo no momento)
Trânsito sentido centro de Ubatuba começa a travar logo depois da praia do Perequê-Mirim. A minha sorte é que tou indo no sentido contrário (e não vamos subir a serra hoje)
Nem me fala! Acabei de entrar no Lázaro e um pouco pra frente (sentido Caraguá) tava começando a travar também... sorte mesmo! 🙏🏼
Ano passado BSkyte morreu Mas esse ano eu não morro

McCandless spent one hour and 55 minutes untethered during this EVA, and the orbital period of the shuttle was just over 90 minutes. So unless I’m missing something, Bruce McCandless *completed a full orbit of Earth while disconnected from the shuttle*. Literal human satellite. 🧪 🔭

In this photo, taken OTD in 1984, Bruce McCandless is an entire football field away from the space shuttle, with no safety tether. No astronaut, before or since, has been that far from their ship while in orbit. 🧪 🔭 Image: NASA
